result.markdown:
# `renv` `renv` is an R package that provides tools for managing R package dependencies. It allows you to create isolated environments for your R projects. By using `renv`, you can: - ensure that your projects are reproducible, with the same package versions used across different environments and collaborators. - update packages when working on new projects without causing conflicts or issues for older projects. This section will guide you through the basics of using renv to manage project dependencies and share your project with others via GitHub. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](images/renv.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## Step 1: Initialize renv First, initialize `renv` in your existing R project: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::init() ``` ::: This will: - Create an isolated environment for your project by setting up a project-specific library inside the `renv` folder. - Install the packages currently being used in your project into this local library. - Generate a `renv.lock` file, which records the versions of all the packages installed. ## Install and manage packages When you want to install a new package within your project, use `renv::install()` **instead of** `install.packages()`: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::install("dplyr") ``` ::: This ensures that the package is installed in your project-specific library. To list all packages installed in your project's environment, use: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::status() ``` ::: ## Step 3: Snapshot Your Project's State Whenever you make changes to your package environment (such as installing or removing packages), update the `renv.lock` file by taking a snapshot of your project’s state: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::snapshot() ``` ::: The `renv.lock` file captures the precise versions of all the packages used in the project. This is crucial for ensuring reproducibility. ::: {.cell layout-align="center" type='info'}

So we have a localised library of packages with our project. But the renv.lock file will only capture packages that are actually be loaded into our environment (so the packages we are using)

::: ## Restore the Environment If you or a collaborator wants to set up the same environment on a different machine, use the `renv.lock` file to recreate the environment: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::restore() ``` ::: This will install all the packages listed in the `renv.lock` file **with the specified versions**, making sure the environment matches the original setup. ## Installing more packages If you need to install more packages or update exisiting ones you can ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::update("dplyr") renv::install("mice") ``` ::: After doing this, re-run the snapshot command: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::snapshot() ``` ::: This will update the `renv.lock` file with the new package versions. ## Issues or errors If you encounter errors where an update or package is breaking your code you can rewind to the previous good state with: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::restor() ``` ::: If you need to roll back changes even further you can view your project states with: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::history() ``` ::: and select good states with: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::revert() ``` ::: ## Ignore renv If you need to ever ignore or bypass `renv` you can. It can be temporarily disabled for a session with: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} renv::deactivate() ``` ::: `renv` does not guarantee complete reproducibility. But it is a significant step in the right direction. What it will not do is directly help manage if you change the version of R you are using (though this is at least recorded in the `renv.lock` file). And if your packages get too old, they could in theory become incompatible with newer versions of R. ### Task Let's try an exercise of installing some packages into a renv environment! ## Greater Reproducibility So `renv` tracks but does not restore the R version you were using for the project. What other options do we have? There is the [rig](https://github.com/r-lib/rig) package that makes it easier to switch between R versions. Alternatively there is [Docker](https://www.docker.com/) - with docker you can package your application and all dependencies into a container.
